questiontext.text: If \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are the roots of \(x^2 - 5x + 6 = 0\), what is the value of \(\alpha^2 + \beta^2\)?

feedback.text: Correct. \(\alpha+\beta=5\), \(\alpha\beta=6\). \(\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta=25-12=13\).
\(25=(\alpha+\beta)^2\), but we need \(\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta=25-12=13\).
12 is \(2\alpha\beta\), which is subtracted, not the answer itself.
\(\alpha^2+\beta^2=(\alpha+\beta)^2-2\alpha\beta=25-12=13\), not 7.

questiontext.text: The average of 5 numbers is 30. If one number equal to 15 is replaced by 55, what is the new average?

feedback.text: Correct. Original sum: \(5\times30=150\). New sum: \(150-15+55=190\). New average: \(190/5=38\).
40 would require new sum = 200; actual new sum = 150-15+55 = 190, giving average 38.
35 would require new sum = 175; the increase from replacing 15 with 55 is +40, giving new sum 190 and average 38.
New sum = 190, so new average = 190/5 = 38, not 41.

questiontext.text: Pipe A fills a tank in 15 hours and Pipe B empties it in 20 hours. If both pipes are opened simultaneously, how many hours will it take to fill the tank (starting from empty)?

feedback.text: Correct. Net filling rate: \(1/15 - 1/20 = 4/60 - 3/60 = 1/60\) per hour. Time to fill: 60 hours.
35 would be the time if the pipes were combined additively (1/15+1/20); but B empties, so the net rate is \(1/15-1/20=1/60\), giving 60 hours.
12 hours = LCM(15,20)/something; the net filling rate gives 60 hours, not 12.

answer.text: The tank can never be filled

feedback.text: Since A's filling rate (1/15) exceeds B's emptying rate (1/20), the net is positive and the tank fills in 60 hours.

questiontext.text: Which of the following is always true for positive real numbers \(a, b\)?

feedback.text: Correct. The AM-GM inequality states that the arithmetic mean is at least the geometric mean for positive reals: \((a+b)/2\geq\sqrt{ab}\), with equality iff \(a=b\).
This is the reverse of AM-GM and is false in general; equality holds only when \(a=b\).
\((a+b)^2/2 = (a^2+2ab+b^2)/2\). We need to compare with \(a^2+b^2\): \((a^2+b^2)-(a^2+2ab+b^2)/2=(a^2+b^2-2ab)/2=(a-b)^2/2\geq0\). So \(a^2+b^2\geq(a+b)^2/2\), meaning this inequality is false.
\((a+b)^2=a^2+2ab+b^2\geq4ab\) iff \((a-b)^2\geq0\), so \((a+b)^2\geq4ab\), not \(\leq4ab\).

questiontext.text: In a certain code, BOOK is written as CNPM. How is DOOR written in the same code?

feedback.text: Correct. The shift pattern from BOOK→CNPM: position 1: +1 (B→C), position 2: −1 (O→N), position 3: +1 (O→P), position 4: +2 (K→M). Applying to DOOR: D+1=E, O−1=N, O+1=P, R+2=T → ENPT.
This does not follow the positional shift pattern (+1,−1,+1,+2) derived from BOOK→CNPM.
Applying the shift pattern (+1,−1,+1,+2) to DOOR gives ENPT, not EPPS.
This does not follow the coding pattern established by BOOK→CNPM.

questiontext.text: A chord of a circle is 8 cm long and is at a distance of 3 cm from the centre. What is the radius of the circle?

feedback.text: Correct. The perpendicular from the centre bisects the chord, creating a right triangle with legs 3 (distance) and 4 (half-chord = 8/2). Radius: \(\sqrt{3^2+4^2}=\sqrt{9+16}=\sqrt{25}=5\) cm.
The radius is \(\sqrt{9+16}=5\) cm; \(\sqrt{9+40}=7\) would require a half-chord of \(\sqrt{40}\approx6.32\), not 4.
This would arise from \(\sqrt{8^2+3^2}=\sqrt{73}\), but the correct approach uses half the chord: \(\sqrt{4^2+3^2}=5\).
4 cm is half the chord length; the radius (hypotenuse of the 3-4-5 right triangle) is 5 cm.

questiontext.text: A boat travels 30 km upstream and 44 km downstream in 10 hours. It also travels 40 km upstream and 55 km downstream in 13 hours. What is the speed of the boat in still water?

feedback.text: Correct. Let upstream speed = \(u\), downstream = \(v\). Equations: \(30/u + 44/v = 10\) and \(40/u + 55/v = 13\). Let \(x=1/u, y=1/v\): \(30x+44y=10\), \(40x+55y=13\). From first: \(x=(10-44y)/30\). Substituting: \(40(10-44y)/30+55y=13\Rightarrow(400-1760y)/30+55y=13\Rightarrow400-1760y+1650y=390\Rightarrow400-110y=390\Rightarrow y=1/11\), so \(v=11\). Then \(x=(10-4)/30=6/30=1/5\), so \(u=5\). Boat speed in still water: \((u+v)/2=(5+11)/2=8\) km/h.
11 km/h is the downstream speed, not the speed in still water; still water speed = (upstream + downstream)/2 = (5+11)/2 = 8 km/h.
5 km/h is the upstream speed; still water speed = (5+11)/2 = 8 km/h.
3 km/h = (11-5)/2 is the stream speed, not the still water speed.

questiontext.text: What is the decimal equivalent of the binary number \((1011010)_2\)?

feedback.text: Correct. \(1011010_2 = 1\times64 + 0\times32 + 1\times16 + 1\times8 + 0\times4 + 1\times2 + 0\times1 = 64+16+8+2=90\).

questiontext.text: Argument: "Students who study more get better grades. Rahul got very good grades. Therefore, Rahul must have studied a lot." This reasoning is an example of

answer.text: Affirming the consequent (a logical fallacy), because good grades could result from other causes besides studying more

feedback.text: Correct. The argument has the form: P→Q, Q, therefore P — this is the fallacy of affirming the consequent. Rahul could have good grades for other reasons (prior knowledge, easy exam, etc.).
Modus ponens has the form P→Q, P, therefore Q — here we affirm P to conclude Q. The argument instead starts with Q (good grades) to conclude P (studied a lot), which is affirming the consequent.
Modus tollens: P→Q, ¬Q, therefore ¬P. This argument does not deny the consequent; it affirms it, making it a fallacy.

answer.text: A valid inductive argument based on empirical evidence

feedback.text: The argument attempts deduction (from premises about studying to a specific conclusion), not induction; and it is invalid due to the fallacy of affirming the consequent.
8 is \(g(3)\); applying \(f\) to this gives \(f(8)=19\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following relations on \(\mathbb{Z}\) (the set of integers) is an equivalence relation?

answer.text: \(a \sim b \iff a - b \text{ is divisible by } 3\)

feedback.text: Correct. This is congruence mod 3: reflexive (\(a-a=0\) divisible by 3), symmetric (if \(3|(a-b)\) then \(3|(b-a)\)), transitive (if \(3|(a-b)\) and \(3|(b-c)\) then \(3|(a-c)\)).
Strict inequality is irreflexive (\(a\not<a\)) and antisymmetric; it is not an equivalence relation.
This is reflexive and symmetric but not transitive: \(1\sim2\) and \(2\sim3\) but \(1\not\sim3\).
This is symmetric and transitive on nonzero integers, but fails reflexivity for \(a=0\) (since \(0\cdot0=0\not>0\)) and is not defined for negative×positive products.

questiontext.text: In what ratio should a 30% alcohol solution be mixed with a 70% alcohol solution to get a 45% alcohol solution?

feedback.text: Correct. By alligation: \(70-45=25\) (quantity of 30% solution) and \(45-30=15\) (quantity of 70% solution). Ratio = 25:15 = 5:3.
The rule of alligation gives 30% : 70% solutions in ratio 25:15 = 5:3, not 3:5.
Equal amounts would give \((30+70)/2=50\%\), not 45%; the required mixture is richer in the 30% solution.

questiontext.text: A student needs to score at least 360 marks in 5 subjects to pass. The student scored 72, 68, 74, and 80 in four subjects. What is the minimum score needed in the fifth subject to pass?

feedback.text: Correct. Sum of four subjects: \(72+68+74+80=294\). Minimum fifth subject score: \(360-294=66\).
\(294+72=366>360\), so 72 is more than needed; the minimum is \(360-294=66\).
\(294+60=354<360\); the student would not pass with 60. The minimum is 66.
\(294+70=364\geq360\), so 70 would work but 66 is the minimum required.

questiontext.text: In how many ways can 6 people be seated around a circular table?

feedback.text: Correct. Circular arrangements of \(n\) people: \((n-1)! = 5! = 120\). One position is fixed (rotational symmetry), and the remaining 5 are arranged in \(5!\) ways.
720 = 6! is the number of linear arrangements; for circular arrangements, divide by \(n\) to account for rotational equivalence: \(6!/6=5!=120\).
60 = (6-1)!/2 applies only when reflections are also considered equivalent (necklace problem); for seated people, distinct rotations are counted, giving 120.
30 = 5!/4 has no standard combinatorial interpretation for this problem; the answer is \((n-1)!=5!=120\).

questiontext.text: The volume of a cone with base radius \(r\) and height \(h\) is \(\frac{1}{3}\pi r^2 h\). If a cone has base radius 6 cm and volume 264 cm³, what is its height? (Use \(\pi \approx \frac{22}{7}\))

feedback.text: Correct. \(\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times36\times h=264\Rightarrow\frac{22\times36}{21}h=264\Rightarrow\frac{792}{21}h=264\Rightarrow h=264\times21/792=5544/792=7\) cm.
With \(h=6\): \(\frac{1}{3}\times\frac{22}{7}\times36\times6=\frac{22\times36\times6}{21}=\frac{4752}{21}\approx226.3\neq264\).
With \(h=8\): \(\frac{22\times36\times8}{21}=\frac{6336}{21}\approx301.7\neq264\).
With \(h=5\): volume \(\approx188.6\neq264\).

questiontext.text: Which letter comes next in the series: B, D, G, K, P, ?

feedback.text: Correct. Differences in alphabetical position: D-B=2, G-D=3, K-G=4, P-K=5, next difference=6: P+6=V (P is the 16th letter, V is the 22nd: 16+6=22).
U is the 21st letter (P=16, +5=21=U); but the pattern of differences is 2,3,4,5,6, so the next is +6, giving V (22nd letter).
W is the 23rd letter (P+7=W); the differences are 2,3,4,5,6 (not 7), so next is P+6=V.
T is the 20th letter (P+4=T); but the gap after P should be 6 (increasing pattern 2,3,4,5,6), giving V.

questiontext.text: If "No P is Q" and "All R is P", which of the following conclusions is valid?

feedback.text: Correct. All R is P (R ⊆ P) and No P is Q (P ∩ Q = ∅). Therefore R ∩ Q ⊆ P ∩ Q = ∅, so No R is Q.
This contradicts the valid conclusion; since No R is Q, no Q is R.
We are told All R is P (R ⊆ P), not the converse; P may contain elements outside R.
"No P is Q" means P and Q are disjoint; "Some P is Q" directly contradicts this premise.

questiontext.text: The distance between the points \((3, -4)\) and \((-5, 3)\) is

feedback.text: \(\sqrt{85}\) would require differences of \(\pm2\) and \(\pm9\) or similar; the actual differences are 8 and 7.
\(|3-(-5)|+|-4-3|=8+7=15\) is the Manhattan distance, not the Euclidean distance \(\sqrt{64+49}=\sqrt{113}\).

questiontext.text: The value of \(\sqrt[3]{125} + \sqrt[3]{-27} + \sqrt[3]{8}\) is

feedback.text: \(5+3+2=10\) ignores the negative sign; \(\sqrt[3]{-27}=-3\), giving sum 4.

questiontext.text: A card is drawn at random from a well-shuffled deck of 52 cards. What is the probability that it is either a king or a red card?

feedback.text: Correct. Red cards: 26. Kings: 4. Red kings: 2. By inclusion-exclusion: \(26+4-2=28\). Probability: \(28/52=7/13\).
\(1/2=26/52\) counts only red cards; adding the two black kings gives \(28/52=7/13\).
\(2/13=8/52\) counts only the 4 kings (or similar small set); the correct count is 28 favorable cards.
